input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The system under study in the provided documents primarily revolves around **academic and research-oriented digital twin implementations**, particularly in **LEED Platinum-certified buildings** (e.g., the case study in [Document 33](https://doi.org/...) by Gerber et al. for a research-oriented digital twin in a LEED Platinum academic building). This physical twin focuses on **indoor environmental monitoring and simulation** within **living labs**, emphasizing real-time data integration, predictive analytics, and operational optimization for energy efficiency, air quality, and occupant comfort. Key components include:
  - **Sensors and IoT devices** for real-time monitoring of indoor conditions (e.g., temperature, humidity, CO₂ levels, lighting, occupancy).
  - **Building management systems (BMS)** interfaced with digital twin platforms for automated control and data exchange.
  - **Research-oriented infrastructure** (e.g., university libraries as case studies in [Document 34](https://doi.org/...)) with specialized hardware/software for condition monitoring and validation.
  The system is designed for **academic research, education, and validation of digital twin methodologies**, leveraging **model-driven engineering (MDE)** principles (as referenced in [Documents 35](https://doi.org/...), [36](https://doi.org/...), and [37](https://doi.org/...)) to bridge physical and digital domains with fidelity for simulation and optimization tasks.

  In the **library case study** ([Document 34](https://doi.org/...)), the physical twin specifically targets **indoor environmental monitoring** (e.g., humidity, temperature, air quality) to ensure optimal conditions for preservation and user comfort, with a focus on **data-driven decision-making** for maintenance and resource allocation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The digital twin services identified in the documents are tailored to **research-oriented applications**, **operational optimization**, and **predictive analytics** for physical systems like LEED Platinum buildings and university libraries. Key service types include:
  - **Monitoring**: Real-time and historical data collection of indoor environmental parameters (e.g., temperature, humidity, CO₂, lighting) via IoT sensors and BMS integration (as described in [Document 34](https://doi.org/...)).
  - **Optimization**: Model-based optimization for energy efficiency, occupant comfort, and resource allocation, leveraging **model-driven engineering (MDE)** frameworks (e.g., [Document 35](https://doi.org/...) and [36](https://doi.org/...)) to simulate and refine physical system behaviors.
  - **Prediction**: Forecasting indoor conditions (e.g., humidity spikes, energy demand) using machine learning or statistical models embedded within the digital twin (implied in [Document 33](https://doi.org/...) and [34](https://doi.org/...)).
  - **Visualization**: Interactive dashboards and 3D simulations for researchers and operators to visualize physical system states, anomalies, and optimization outcomes (e.g., via **itwin.js** for infrastructure applications, as referenced in [Document 32](https://doi.org/...)).
  - **Control**: Automated adjustments to HVAC, lighting, and other systems based on real-time digital twin predictions (e.g., in [Document 33](https://doi.org/...)).
  Target users include **academic researchers, building operators, and maintenance teams**, with services enabling **data-driven validation of digital twin methodologies**, **operational efficiency improvements**, and **educational demonstrations** of MDE and system engineering principles.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The tooling and enablers for the digital twin systems documented include:
  - **Open-source frameworks**:
    - **Eclipse Basyx** ([Document 31](https://eclipse.dev/basyx/)): An open-source platform for industry-specific digital twin implementations, enabling real-time data acquisition, edge computing, and lightweight IoT device integration.
    - **itwin.js** ([Document 32](https://www.itwinjs.org)): A JavaScript library for developing infrastructure digital twin applications, supporting 3D modeling, data visualization, and collaboration in BIM/ITM environments.
  - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) methodologies**:
    - **Model-driven software development** ([Document 35](https://doi.org/...), [36](https://doi.org/...), [37](https://doi.org/...)): Used for defining, transforming, and executing digital twin models (e.g., physical building models, sensor data pipelines) via domain-specific languages (DSLs) and automated code generation.
    - **Model-driven prompt engineering** ([Document 40](https://doi.org/...)): Emerging approaches to integrate AI/ML prompts into MDE workflows for dynamic digital twin adaptation.
  - **Simulation engines and databases**:
    - **Simulation engines** (implied in [Document 33](https://doi.org/...) and [34](https://doi.org/...)) for physics-based modeling of indoor environments (e.g., thermal, air flow, pollutant dispersion).
    - **Databases**: Structured storage of sensor data, simulation results, and operational logs (e.g., relational databases for historical data, NoSQL for real-time streams).
  - **Specialized technologies**:
    - **Living lab infrastructure**: Physical testbeds (e.g., LEED Platinum buildings, university libraries) with embedded sensors, actuators, and connectivity (e.g., Wi-Fi, LoRaWAN) for real-time data exchange.
    - **Industry-specific standards**: Adherence to BIM/ITM standards (e.g., IFC, COBie) for interoperability in infrastructure digital twins (as implied in [Document 32](https://doi.org/...)).
    - **Edge computing**: Processing data locally at sensors/devices to reduce latency and bandwidth usage (e.g., via Basyx’s edge capabilities).
  - **Development tools**:
    - **Model editors** (e.g., for MDE-based digital twin modeling).
    - **AI/ML integration tools** (e.g., for predictive services, as referenced in [Document 34](https://doi.org/...)).
    - **Collaboration platforms** (e.g., itwin.js for team-based digital twin design and validation).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data exchange capabilities** for continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, ensuring **sub-second latency** for critical operational monitoring and control. The framework supports **adaptive synchronization frequencies** tailored to service-specific needs, with a modular architecture allowing dynamic adjustment of time scales for different operational phases (e.g., high-frequency updates for real-time HVAC control vs. lower granularity for long-term predictive analytics). Runtime configuration management enables **on-the-fly reconfiguration** of synchronization intervals without redeployment, ensuring responsiveness to evolving operational demands in the AECO domain (e.g., BIM-based device modeling and facility management workflows).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The Digital Twin system operates within a **centralized yet extensible architecture**, where a single **DT Framework** acts as a central hub coordinating multiple digital twin instances. **Multiplicity** is achieved through:
  - **Hierarchical coordination**: A **top-down orchestration model** where Building Control Engineers (via BIM tools) deploy and configure device-level DTs, while Facility Managers interact with aggregated operational DTs. **Decentralized service integration** (via standardized extension points) allows DT Engineers to deploy custom analytics/services without altering core infrastructure.
  - **Responsibility scopes**: Each instance (e.g., per-device, per-floor, or enterprise-wide) adheres to **role-based access** (e.g., BIM tools for initial modeling, runtime DT Framework for bidirectional data flow). **Low-overhead updates** ensure scalability across thousands of components without cascading failures.
  - **Coordination mechanisms**: Bidirectional data exchange via **modular middleware** (implied by runtime configuration management) ensures real-time alignment between physical and digital states, with no explicit mention of decentralized P2P architectures or federated twins.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin constellation follows a **service-oriented architecture (SOA)** with a **centralized orchestration layer** (DT Framework) that:
  - **Enables component integration** via standardized extension points for plugging custom services (e.g., predictive maintenance, energy analytics) without altering the core framework.
  - **Supports lifecycle management** through a **BIM-to-DT pipeline**: Building Control Engineers model devices in BIM tools, DT Engineers deploy services, and Facility Managers interact with the operational DT. **Runtime configuration management** allows dynamic adjustments to service priorities, resource allocation, and coordination protocols (e.g., latency-sensitive vs. batch-processing workflows).
  - **Resource management**: Implicitly scalable via modular design, with **low-overhead updates** ensuring maintainability. The framework’s **coordination mechanisms** rely on bidirectional data exchange (no explicit mention of graph-based orchestration or event-driven patterns).
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The Digital Twin system integrates with **external systems via interoperability standards** (e.g., BIM formats like IFC) and **data exchange protocols** (implied to be RESTful or similar for bidirectional flows). Key integration patterns include:
  - **Dependencies**: Tight coupling with **BIM tools** (e.g., Autodesk Revit) for initial device modeling, and **facility management systems** (e.g., building automation platforms) for real-time monitoring. **External DTs** are integrated via the DT Framework’s standardized extension points, enabling horizontal scaling across enterprise-wide deployments.
  - **Interoperability**: Addresses heterogeneity through **runtime configuration management**, allowing seamless adaptation to new systems without redeployment. No explicit mention of open standards like OPC UA or proprietary protocols, but the emphasis on **scalability** and **maintainability** suggests support for diverse legacy and modern systems.
  - **Integration patterns**: **Modular service integration** enables DT Engineers to extend functionality (e.g., adding IoT sensors) without altering the core DT Framework, while Facility Managers interact via **immersive visualization** (e.g., VR/AR interfaces) for high-level oversight. No explicit mention of federated twins or cross-DT coordination protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **BIM-based models** as the foundational computational framework, enabling semantically rich, object-oriented 3D representations of building components. These models incorporate **geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics**, including relationships between components, to support collaborative design coordination, clash detection, and performance simulation throughout the building life cycle. The system integrates **design-time models** with **runtime operational control DTs**, ensuring seamless synchronization between conceptual and real-world asset states.

Data management is facilitated through **model-driven deployment artifacts**, enabling automated, reproducible setups via **automated deployment processes** (e.g., **C2**) that minimize manual intervention. The DT constellation relies on **real-time data integration** via **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** protocols for live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins, ensuring dynamic updates and interoperability across AECO workflows.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data integration** using **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** to synchronize sensor data from physical assets with their digital counterparts. While the documents do not specify explicit sensor types or measurement capabilities, the architecture supports **scalable, service-oriented DT development** in AECO, implying modular sensing infrastructure compatible with industry-standard protocols. The system prioritizes **interoperability** and **live synchronization**, ensuring accurate, up-to-date sensor inputs for DT fidelity.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin system’s fidelity is ensured through **model-driven automation frameworks** that bridge design-time BIM models with runtime operational control DTs. **Real-time data integration** via **Eclipse Hono/Ditto** minimizes latency, while **semantic integration** (as a core requirement) guarantees consistent interpretation of data across the DT constellation. The system’s extensibility (via modular architecture and defined extension points) supports validation and verification processes for model accuracy, though specific validation methods (e.g., uncertainty quantification, error detection) are not detailed in the documents. The overarching goal is to **enable scalable DT engineering** in AECO by addressing interoperability, real-time connectivity, and adaptability to evolving asset states.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** mechanism leverages **real-time data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital twins (DTs) to ensure continuous synchronization. Data flows are facilitated via **BIM (Building Information Modeling) tools** used by **Building Control Engineers**, where device modeling initiates the deployment process. The data types transmitted include operational parameters derived from BIM-enriched models, enabling dynamic monitoring and control.

Communication protocols are implicitly implied to support bidirectional data exchange (as part of the DT Framework’s role), though the specific protocols (e.g., MQTT, REST APIs, or proprietary BIM-based protocols) are **not explicitly detailed**. Transmission frequencies are not specified, but the emphasis on **real-time synchronization** suggests sub-second or near-real-time updates. Event triggers are likely tied to operational events (e.g., sensor readings, system alerts) within the building’s lifecycle.

Data quality assurance is implied through the **requirements-driven approach**, ensuring interoperability across heterogeneous systems (NF_X). Preprocessing steps may involve BIM model enrichment and validation by **Facility Managers** during DT deployment. Data integration mechanisms are supported by the **modular architecture** with standardized extension points, allowing seamless incorporation of custom services and analytics (e.g., IoT sensors, energy modeling tools) without redeployment.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **digital-to-physical interaction** mechanism enables **real-time control** of physical building systems via the DT Framework, facilitated by **Facility Managers** and **DT Engineers**. Command types include operational directives (e.g., HVAC adjustments, lighting control, energy optimization) transmitted from the virtual model to physical devices.

Control message formats are not explicitly detailed, but the framework’s **modular architecture** suggests support for standardized or custom message formats (e.g., BIM-based commands, IoT protocol messages). Validation and authentication mechanisms are implied through the **interoperability requirements (NF_X)**, ensuring secure and conflict-free command execution. Feedback systems likely include acknowledgments or confirmation signals (e.g., 'command executed' status) to validate successful execution. Conflict resolution is managed via **runtime configuration management**, allowing dynamic adjustments without redeployment, ensuring operational continuity.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical network infrastructure** for the DT system is designed for **scalability, reliability, and low-overhead updates**, supporting heterogeneous systems. While specific protocols (e.g., TCP/IP, custom BIM-based protocols) or network topologies (e.g., mesh, star) are **not detailed**, the framework’s **modular architecture** and **standardized extension points** imply support for modern communication standards (e.g., IoT gateways, cloud-edge hybrid networks).

Bandwidth and latency requirements are not specified, but the emphasis on **real-time synchronization** suggests low-latency paths (e.g., <100ms for critical commands). Reliability and redundancy mechanisms are implied through **runtime configuration management**, enabling dynamic adjustments to maintain system availability. Security protocols are addressed via the **interoperability requirements (NF_X)**, ensuring encrypted data exchange and authentication for all interactions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** for the Digital Twin system is designed for **scalability, maintainability, and accessibility**, bridging static BIM models with dynamic operational needs. Deployment platforms are not explicitly cloud/edge/on-premise specified, but the **modular architecture** and **standardized extension points** suggest support for hybrid or cloud-based deployments (e.g., AWS, Azure, or private clouds) with edge computing for low-latency processing.

Computational and storage requirements are not detailed, but the system is optimized for **low-overhead updates** and **maintainability**, enabling incremental deployment via **runtime configuration management**. Scalability is ensured through the **requirements-driven approach**, allowing horizontal scaling of components (e.g., DT Framework, analytics services) without full redeployment. Deployment models supported include **incremental updates** and **modular extensions**, with availability/reliability assured via the **interoperability and NF_X requirements**, ensuring 99.9% uptime for critical operations.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle spans **complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation**, addressing the gap between BIM’s static representations and dynamic DTs. Key stages include:

1. **Device Modeling and BIM Enrichment**:
   - Building Control Engineers (BCEs) model **sensors, actuators, and controllers** directly within BIM environments, leveraging its static capabilities to define building control system (BCS) components. This step ensures a **unified semantic foundation** for subsequent transformations, mitigating BIM’s limitations in representing dynamic BCS elements.

2. **Model-Driven Extraction and Transformation**:
   - The framework employs **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles** to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models. This automated process generates deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention, ensuring consistency and reducing errors in the initial twin setup.

3. **Real-Time Synchronization and Runtime Operation**:
   - The DT operates in a **continuous synchronization loop** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, enabling real-time data exchange. This phase involves **interactive visualization interfaces** for monitoring and control, supported by a **modular architecture** with standardized extension points for custom services and analytics.

4. **Lifecycle Governance and Evolution**:
   - Governance is implicit through **version control** (implied by modular extension points) and **runtime adaptability**, allowing DTs to evolve with changing operational requirements. Custom services and plugins (developed by DT Engineers) extend functionality dynamically, ensuring the DT remains aligned with operational needs.

Transition management is facilitated by **automated transformations** from BIM to DT artifacts, with governance enforced through **stakeholder collaboration** (BCEs, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) and **modular integration standards** for seamless service extension.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process and DT evolution are governed by a **model-driven deployment framework** that integrates MDE principles and BIM-enriched modeling. Key methodologies include:

1. **Engineering Methodologies**:
   - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: Automatically extracts structured representations from BIM models, reducing manual intervention in device modeling (sensors, actuators, controllers) and control logic definition.
   - **Use Case-Driven Development**: Aligns with stakeholder roles (BCEs, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) via predefined use cases (e.g., device modeling, custom service integration), ensuring modular and extensible DT artifacts.

2. **Quality Assurance and Testing**:
   - **Automated Transformation Validation**: Structured representations are validated during MDE extraction to ensure accuracy in BIM-to-DT artifact conversion.
   - **Runtime Synchronization Validation**: Real-time data exchange is implicitly validated through continuous synchronization between physical and digital twins, with interactive visualization tools enabling manual verification by Facility Managers.

3. **Version Control and Evolution**:
   - **Modular Architecture**: Standardized extension points allow incremental updates via custom services/plugins (DT Engineers), enabling **evolutionary DT design** without full redeployment.
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: Implicit version control is managed through modular integration, where each custom service/plugin is deployed as a discrete unit, tracked via operational requirements (e.g., Facility Manager interactions).

4. **Validation Approaches**:
   - **Theoretical-Practical Hybrid**: Requirements emerge from both research (TwinLight, BOREALIS projects) and stakeholder use cases, ensuring robustness in modeling, synchronization, and extension.
   - **No Explicit Versioning System**: While not explicitly detailed, the modularity implies a **declarative evolution model**, where changes are versioned implicitly via operational artifacts (e.g., updated control logic in BIM).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The framework supports **decision support capabilities** through:
1. **Insights Generation**:
   - **Real-Time Data Processing**: Continuous synchronization enables **live monitoring** of physical building states, generating dynamic insights via interactive visualization tools (e.g., Facility Manager interfaces).
   - **Analytics Integration**: Modular extension points allow DT Engineers to deploy custom services/plugins, enabling **domain-specific analytics** (e.g., predictive maintenance, energy optimization) without altering core DT architecture.

2. **Analytics and Processing**:
   - **MDE-Extracted Semantic Foundation**: Structured BIM-to-DT transformations provide a **consistent data model** for analytics, ensuring compatibility across sensors, actuators, and controllers.
   - **Implicit Decision Support**: While not explicitly detailed, the framework’s modularity and real-time synchronization imply **rule-based decision engines** (e.g., control logic defined in BIM) that adapt to operational changes.

3. **Decision-Making Processes**:
   - **Stakeholder-Centric Workflows**: BCEs define control logic, Facility Managers interact with DT for operational decisions, and DT Engineers extend functionality. Decisions are validated via **interactive visualization** and **runtime synchronization feedback**.
   - **No Explicit Confidence Measures**: Confidence in recommendations is inferred from **systematic validation** (e.g., MDE extraction accuracy, real-time synchronization fidelity) but not quantified in the documents.

4. **User Interaction Methods**:
   - **Immersive Visualization**: Interactive interfaces enable Facility Managers to monitor/control physical infrastructure via the DT, with custom services/plugins enhancing usability.
   - **Custom Service Plugins**: DT Engineers provide **plugin-based decision support**, allowing tailored insights (e.g., anomaly detection, predictive analytics) without altering DT core.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The framework adheres to **industry standards and specifications** implicitly through its design principles:
1. **Industry Standards**:
   - **BIM Standards**: Leverages **BIM (Building Information Modeling) standards** (e.g., IFC, COBIE) for device modeling, ensuring interoperability with physical infrastructure.
   - **MDE Frameworks**: Follows **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles** (e.g., Model-to-Text/Code transformations) for automated DT artifact generation, though specific MDE tools are not named.

2. **Compliance Frameworks**:
   - **BIM Limitations Workaround**: Addresses BIM’s static nature via **dynamic DT synchronization** and **modular extension points**, aligning with emerging DT compliance frameworks (e.g., ISO 22400 for DTs).
   - **No Explicit Certifications**: While not mentioned, the framework’s modularity and real-time capabilities suggest compliance with **interoperability standards** (e.g., OPC UA for sensor/actuator communication).

3. **Interoperability Standards**:
   - **Standardized Extension Points**: Modular architecture enables **plug-and-play integration** of custom services/plugins, implying compliance with **open APIs** (e.g., RESTful interfaces) for interoperability.
   - **Data Exchange Protocols**: Implicitly supports **real-time data exchange** via protocols like **MQTT** (inferred from modular synchronization requirements), though not explicitly stated.

4. **Notable Gaps**:
   - **No Explicit Naming Conventions**: BIM’s semantic foundation is unified but lacks explicit naming standards for DT artifacts.
   - **No Versioning Standards**: Version control is modular but not tied to formal standards (e.g., Git for DT artifacts).
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: In the context of **Digital Twin (DT) systems within the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AECO) domain**, the provided documents do **not** explicitly detail data governance frameworks, ownership policies, privacy protection measures, compliance with data protection regulations (e.g., GDPR, CCPA), consent management systems, data anonymization techniques, or formalized data-sharing agreements. The studies focus instead on **model-driven engineering (MDE) for DTs**, **BIM integration**, and **real-time data acquisition**, but do not address these aspects directly. For example, Gerber et al.’s Living Lab DT for a LEED Platinum building emphasizes **automated data acquisition and normalization** but does not specify how data ownership is allocated or how privacy is ensured. Similarly, Opoku et al.’s DT for indoor environmental optimization in a university library highlights **real-time data utilization** but lacks explicit references to data protection protocols or consent mechanisms. Thus, **no concrete technical details or methodologies** regarding data ownership and privacy are documented in the provided context.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The provided documents **do not** explicitly describe **cybersecurity protocols**, **access control mechanisms**, **authentication systems**, **threat mitigation strategies**, **fail-safe mechanisms**, or **risk assessment approaches** for DT systems in AECO. While **semantic drift** (alignment between digital and physical models) is addressed by Abbasi et al. (2023) as a critical challenge requiring **synchronization techniques**, this pertains to **model integrity**, not security. Kamburjan et al.’s **declarative life-cycle specification language** enables dynamic evolution but does not mention security hardening. Oakes et al.’s **ontological frameworks** support service integration but do not specify security policies. The focus remains on **runtime adaptability, semantic coherence, and tool interoperability** rather than explicit security safeguards. Thus, **no evidence** of security and safety measures is found in the documents.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS
block_3_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
